title: Reupholstering a piano stool
subtitle: Re-cover a drop-in padded seat with new fabric and fresh foam
excerpt: How to strip and re-cover the padded seat on a drop-in piano stool: removing the old cover, replacing tired foam, wrapping with dacron, cutting the new fabric, and stapling it for a tight finish.

glossaryTerms.definition: A padded seat board that sits in a rebate in a chair or stool frame and lifts out without any fixings. The frame holds the pad by weight and rebate depth alone. Drop-in pads are the easiest upholstery work: the entire re-cover is done with the pad on the bench, away from the frame.

content.text: is the most accessible upholstery project: the pad lifts out, all work is done on the bench, and the result is immediately visible. Replace the foam if it is flat or misshapen; worn foam will show through any new cover fabric.

Lift the pad from the stool. Turn it upside down and remove all staples and tacks with the staple remover, working around the perimeter. Pull the old cover fabric off the pad board. If the underside has a dust cover (black or white woven fabric), remove this too.

Peel or cut off the old foam from the pad board. Cut a piece of CMHR foam to the exact size of the board top using the upholstery shears; CMHR foam cuts cleanly with sharp shears. A 50 mm medium-density foam is appropriate for a piano stool; 25 mm is too thin and will compress to nothing within a year. Secure the foam to the board with a thin smear of contact adhesive around the top edges only.

Cut a piece of dacron wrap approximately 100 mm larger than the pad on all four sides. Lay it over the foam and smooth it over the pad edges without compressing the foam. Staple the dacron to the underside of the board at 100 mm intervals, keeping it taut but not distorting the foam shape. Trim the excess dacron to within 20 mm of the staple line.
Step 4, cut and apply the cover fabric

Cut the cover fabric with the upholstery shears to the pad board size plus 60 mm on all four sides. Lay the fabric face-down on the bench, place the pad foam-side-down in the centre, and pull the fabric over the first side. Place one staple at each midpoint of all four sides to anchor the fabric, then check the face for puckers and wrinkles before completing. Work outward from the centre on each side, pulling the fabric firm and stapling at 40 mm intervals. Cut mitred folds at each corner and staple them flat.

attrs.symptom: Cover fabric puckers or creases across the top
attrs.cause: Fabric was pulled unevenly, or the first staple row on one side was tighter than the other.
attrs.fix: Remove all staples and start again from the midpoint anchors, pulling equal tension on opposite sides before stapling.

attrs.symptom: Pad does not sit flat in the stool rebate after recovering
attrs.cause: The dacron or foam extends too far over the edge and is adding height on one side.
attrs.fix: Trim the dacron to the exact edge of the board and re-check. If the foam is oversized, trim it back 2 mm on the affected sides.
